title: Non-Uniqueness and Flexibility in Two-Dimensional Euler Equations
speaker: Elia Bruè
abstract: In 1962, Yudovich established the well-posedness of the two-dimensional incompressible Euler equations for solutions with bounded vorticity. However, uniqueness within the broader class of solutions with L^p vorticity remains a key unresolved question. In this talk, I will survey recent advances on this problem and present new nonuniqueness results, obtained via the convex integration method. This work is in collaboration with Colombo and Kumar.

proof_stages.label: Vorticity formulation and the finite-Lp uniqueness problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up 2D incompressible Euler on the torus, rewrites it as a vorticity active-scalar equation, recalls LP a priori heuristics and Yudovich well-posedness for bounded vorticity, and isolates uniqueness for finite-p vorticity as the central open problem.

proof_stages.label: Prior negative evidence through symmetry breaking
proof_stages.summary: Known and conjectural nonuniqueness scenarios are reviewed, especially forced radial symmetry breaking and singular-data regularization scenarios, to motivate looking for flexible weak solutions in 2D Euler.

proof_stages.label: Flexibility targets and Euler-Reynolds relaxation
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls wild Euler phenomena and introduces the Euler-Reynolds relaxed system as the framework in which convex integration starts from subsolutions and iteratively drives the Reynolds stress to zero.

proof_stages.label: Periodic high-frequency perturbations and quadratic cancellation
proof_stages.summary: The standard convex-integration step is described: add a high-frequency building block with a slowly varying amplitude so that the quadratic self-interaction cancels the previous Reynolds stress after averaging, while remaining errors are inverted through the divergence.

proof_stages.label: L1 vorticity obstruction for the standard scheme
proof_stages.summary: Earlier 2D Euler convex-integration constructions only reached weak-L1 or subintegral vorticity because L2 normalization of periodic building blocks forces an L1 gradient cost of order the frequency, and the small stress amplitude cannot compensate for that loss.

proof_stages.label: Main small-supercritical Lp flexibility and nonuniqueness theorem
proof_stages.summary: The new result states that for p slightly larger than one there are 2D Euler solutions with vorticity in LP that can approximately prescribe initial and final velocities and yield infinitely many solutions for many initial data, with nonconservation of energy and no vanishing-viscosity selection.

proof_stages.label: Asynchronous spatial inhomogeneity principle from transport
proof_stages.summary: The key conceptual input comes from sharp nonuniqueness for linear transport equations, where acting in highly localized and asynchronous regions beats the integrability range of standard homogeneous convex integration.

proof_stages.label: Nonperiodic travelling-jet implementation for Euler
proof_stages.summary: The Euler construction replaces spatially periodic waves by compact jets moving along trajectories, uses time averaging and variable speed/size to reconstruct the Reynolds stress, and relies on precise compactly supported almost-Euler dipole profiles.

examples.name: Forced radial symmetry breaking for 2D Euler
examples.note: Vishik's forced problem with zero initial vorticity gives both a radial solution and a non-radial solution, serving as concrete evidence for nonuniqueness mechanisms rather than as part of the new construction.
examples.engagement_reason: he states the forced radial nonuniqueness/symmetry-breaking theorem and describes its radial versus angular solutions and instability mechanism without carrying out the construction or computations

passes.reason: Radial forcing and symmetric zero data are used to produce a nonradial second solution, refuting uniqueness/symmetry preservation.
zero initial vorticity with radial forcing is a symmetric degenerate setup admitting both radial and non-radial solutions, refuting uniqueness/symmetry inheritance
Radial forcing with zero initial vorticity is highly symmetric and trivial at the datum, and the nonradial solution is used to disprove uniqueness/symmetry inheritance for forced Euler.
A rotationally symmetric forcing with zero initial vorticity yields both a radial and a non-radial solution, disproving uniqueness in the forced problem.
A rotationally invariant forcing with zero initial vorticity produces a nonradial second solution, contradicting uniqueness/symmetry propagation.
Zero initial vorticity with radial forcing yields two distinct solutions, one radial and one not, breaking uniqueness despite symmetric data.
radial forcing with symmetric data yields two forced Euler solutions, one non-radial, so it refutes uniqueness for the forced problem.
Zero initial vorticity with radial forcing admits both a radial and a nonradial solution, defeating uniqueness/symmetry preservation.
radial forcing with the degenerate zero datum yields both a radial and a non-radial solution to the same forced Euler problem
Used to refute uniqueness for forced 2D Euler from zero initial vorticity, with radially symmetric data/force but a nonradial solution.
Radial forcing with the same zero initial data yields both a radial and a nonradial solution, refuting uniqueness for the forced Euler problem.
Radial forcing from the zero vorticity datum yields both radial and non-radial solutions, so it is a nonuniqueness counterexample with symmetric data and a degenerate initial state.

examples.name: Two regularizations of a singular polar initial vorticity
examples.note: A conjectural scenario compares two bounded cutoffs of the same power-law singular initial datum, illustrating how different limiting behaviors could produce unforced nonuniqueness.
examples.engagement_reason: he writes the singular polar vorticity form, describes two concrete epsilon-regularizations of the origin, and traces the differing expected rotations of the resulting Yudovich solutions

passes.reason: The two cutoffs of the same singular polar vorticity are presented as a numerical/conjectural route to different Euler evolutions from the same singular datum.
the two ε-regularizations approach a power-law singular polar datum as a limiting boundary case and are presented as a numerical nonuniqueness scenario
The power-law polar datum is a singular limiting case, and the two cutoffs are presented as a numerical/conjectural enemy scenario for uniqueness.
The two cutoffs probe a singular power-law polar datum as a limiting case and are presented as a numerical/conjectural nonuniqueness scenario.
The two cutoffs of the same singular polar datum are presented as a proposed/numerical nonuniqueness mechanism yielding different limiting behaviors.
The epsilon cutoffs are limiting regularizations of a singular polar datum and are presented as a putative/numerical nonuniqueness scenario.
the two cutoffs of a scale-invariant point-singular polar vorticity are presented as a conjectural nonuniqueness mechanism from a limiting boundary datum.
The two cutoffs of a scale-invariant singular polar datum are presented as a proposed numerical enemy case for uniqueness.
the singular polar datum with ε-cutoff regularizations is a limiting setup, but the two different evolutions are presented only as a numerical/nonrigorous scenario
A conjectural numerical nonuniqueness scenario from two cutoffs of a homogeneous point-singular datum, a limiting scaling-type object.
The two cutoffs form a numerically supported singular-limit scenario rather than a proved counterexample, and the underlying datum is a limiting power-law polar singularity.
The two cutoffs of a power-law singular polar datum are presented as a conjectural/numerical enemy to uniqueness, and the singular datum is a limiting boundary object.

examples.name: Compactly supported wild Euler solution

examples.name: Prescribed kinetic-energy profile
examples.note: The De Lellis-Székelyhidi theorem that continuous Euler flows can realize a chosen energy profile exemplifies convex-integration flexibility already after the general idea has been stated.
examples.engagement_reason: he states the convex-integration result that an arbitrary continuous kinetic-energy profile can be prescribed, but does not work through a particular profile

passes.reason: A weak Euler solution with arbitrary prescribed kinetic energy is used to show that energy conservation fails in that weak class.
weak Euler solutions with arbitrary nonconstant prescribed kinetic energy profiles refute energy conservation in that weak class
Solutions with an arbitrary nonconstant kinetic-energy profile are used to refute energy conservation for low-regularity Euler solutions.

Allowing an arbitrary continuous kinetic-energy profile shows failure of energy conservation/rigidity for weak Euler solutions.
Prescribing an arbitrary nonconstant kinetic-energy profile gives weak Euler solutions contradicting smooth energy conservation.
continuous Euler solutions with an arbitrary prescribed, hence possibly nonconstant, energy profile show failure of energy conservation in that weak class.

A weak Euler solution with arbitrary prescribed energy profile refutes energy conservation in that weak class, but the profile itself need not be structurally special.
Prescribing an arbitrary nonconstant kinetic-energy profile disproves energy conservation in the continuous weak Euler class.
Choosing a nonconstant prescribed energy profile gives weak Euler solutions violating classical energy conservation, while the profile itself is arbitrary rather than structurally special.

examples.name: Asynchronous four-square transport mixing
examples.note: The transport construction breaks and redistributes pieces in a highly asynchronous way, and the speaker explicitly uses this example to motivate eliminating spatial periodicity in the Euler convex-integration perturbations.
examples.engagement_reason: he draws and reasons through the four-square transport process, breaking squares, moving subcubes, discussing weak convergence and asynchronous timing in detail

passes.reason: The asynchronous four-square mixing construction is used to obtain sharp-range nonuniqueness for transport, with no intrinsic sporadic or symmetric structure.
the asynchronous dyadic square mixing is engineered to reach the sharp transport nonuniqueness range and thus serves against transport uniqueness
The asynchronous dyadic mixing construction is optimized to reach the sharp transport nonuniqueness range and gives a nontrivial evolution from constant density.
The asynchronous dyadic four-square mixing scheme is optimized to reach the sharp nonuniqueness range for rough transport.
The asynchronous four-square mixing construction gives nonunique transport dynamics in the sharp integrability range.
The asynchronous four-square mixing construction attains the sharp transport nonuniqueness range, countering well-posedness beyond the known threshold.
the asynchronous dyadic four-square mixing construction is used to reach the sharp nonuniqueness range for Sobolev transport.
The asynchronous four-square cascade is an optimized sharp-range construction showing transport well-posedness can fail beyond the DiPerna-Lions regime.
the asynchronous dyadic square mixing attains the sharp transport-integrability range while producing a nonunique transport evolution
The asynchronous four-square transport construction is used against well-posedness and is optimized to reach the sharp nonuniqueness exponent range.
The asynchronous transport mixing gives nonunique evolution in the sharp rough-transport exponent range, defeating uniqueness beyond the well-posed threshold.
The asynchronous four-square mixing construction is used to prove sharp-range transport nonuniqueness, making it a critical extremal construction rather than a symmetric periodic one.
